Description: Organize microservices into three layers: transport decodes requests and encodes responses; endpoint adapts transport to service; service contains business logic.
Do: Separate transport
Dont: endpoint
Code Good: and service into distinct packages or files.
Code Bad: Do not mix HTTP decoding logic with business rules in the same function.

Description: Define the service as a plain Go interface with context as the first parameter on every method.
Do: Define a Go interface for your service contract and implement it with a concrete struct.
Dont: Do not embed framework types into your service; keep business logic framework-free.

Description: Wrap each service method in an endpoint.Endpoint to create a uniform function signature for middleware.
Do: Create one endpoint per service method and apply middleware like logging and instrumentation.
Dont: Do not call service methods directly from transport; always go through endpoints for middleware support.

Description: Use go-kit transport packages to bind endpoints to HTTP routes or gRPC service definitions.
Do: Use httptransport.NewServer for HTTP and grpctransport.NewServer for gRPC to bind endpoints.
Dont: Do not write raw HTTP handlers when using go-kit; use the transport layer for consistent error encoding.
Code Good:
httpHandler := httptransport.NewServer(
    endpoints.PlaceOrder,
    decodeHTTPPlaceOrderRequest,
    encodeHTTPResponse,
    httptransport.ServerErrorEncoder(encodeError),
)
grpcHandler := grpctransport.NewServer(
    endpoints.PlaceOrder,
    decodeGRPCPlaceOrderRequest,
    encodeGRPCPlaceOrderResponse,
)

Description: Wrap service calls with a circuit breaker to stop cascading failures when a downstream service is unhealthy.
Do: Use gobreaker.NewCircuitBreaker with sensible thresholds and wrap outbound calls.
Dont: Do not call failing services indefinitely; circuit breakers fail fast and let services recover.

Description: Register services and discover instances dynamically so clients do not need hardcoded addresses.
Do: Use a service registry like Consul and integrate it with go-kit sd package.
Dont: Do not hardcode service URLs; use dynamic discovery for resilience and scaling.
Code Good:
client, err := api.NewClient(api.DefaultConfig())
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
instancer := consulsd.NewInstancer(
    consul.NewClient(client),
    logger,
    "payment-service",
    []string{"production"},
    true,
)
endpointer := sd.NewEndpointer(instancer, factory, logger)
balancer := lb.NewRoundRobin(endpointer)
endpoint := lb.Retry(3, 5*time.Second, balancer)

Description: Use client-side load balancing to distribute requests across discovered service instances.
Do: Use lb.NewRoundRobin or lb.NewRandom with sd.NewEndpointer for client-side balancing.
Dont: Do not rely solely on DNS for load balancing; it lacks health awareness and has TTL caching issues.

Description: Instrument services with OpenTelemetry to trace requests across service boundaries.
Do: Initialize a TracerProvider and create spans at service boundaries and key operations.
Dont: Do not rely solely on logs for debugging distributed systems; traces show request flow.
Code Good:
exporter, err := otlptracegrpc.New(ctx, otlptracegrpc.WithEndpoint("otel-collector:4317"), otlptracegrpc.WithInsecure())
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
tp := sdktrace.NewTracerProvider(
    sdktrace.WithBatcher(exporter),
    sdktrace.WithResource(resource.NewWithAttributes(semconv.SchemaURL, semconv.ServiceName("order-service"))),
)
otel.SetTracerProvider(tp)
tracer := otel.Tracer("order-service")
ctx, span := tracer.Start(ctx, "PlaceOrder")
defer span.End()

Description: Expose /healthz and /readyz endpoints so orchestrators can check service health and readiness.
Do: Implement separate liveness and readiness probes that check dependencies.
Dont: Do not use a single health endpoint that always returns 200; check actual dependency health.

Description: Load configuration from environment variables and config files following the 12-factor app methodology.
Do: Use viper to read from env vars with fallback to config files and set sensible defaults.
Dont: Do not hardcode configuration values; externalize them for different environments.

Description: Use slog with structured fields and a correlation ID to trace log entries across services.
Do: Create a logger with service name and pass request-scoped fields via context.
Dont: Do not use fmt.Println or unstructured log.Printf; structured logs are queryable.

Description: Expose Prometheus metrics for request count, latency, and error rate to monitor service health.
Do: Register counters and histograms and update them in middleware.
Dont: Do not run services without metrics; you cannot know if a service is degrading without measurements.

Description: Use an API gateway or reverse proxy to route external traffic to internal services and handle cross-cutting concerns.
Do: Use httputil.ReverseProxy or a dedicated gateway for routing and rate limiting.
Dont: Do not expose every microservice directly to the internet; use a gateway for unified access.
Code Good:
target, _ := url.Parse("http://order-service:8080")
proxy := httputil.NewSingleHostReverseProxy(target)
proxy.ModifyResponse = func(resp *http.Response) error {
    resp.Header.Set("X-Gateway", "api-gw")
    return nil
}
mux := http.NewServeMux()
mux.Handle("/api/orders/", http.StripPrefix("/api", proxy))
log.Fatal(http.ListenAndServe(":9090", mux))

Description: Use asynchronous messaging for event-driven communication between services to decouple producers from consumers.
Do: Use NATS JetStream or Kafka for durable event publishing and subscribing.
Dont: Do not use synchronous HTTP calls for fire-and-forget events; messaging decouples services.

Description: Implement the saga pattern to coordinate multi-service transactions with compensating actions on failure.
Do: Define each step with an execute and a compensate function; run compensations in reverse on failure.
Dont: Do not use distributed locks or two-phase commit across microservices; they do not scale.

Description: Retry transient failures with exponential backoff and jitter to avoid thundering herd problems.
Do: Use exponential backoff with jitter and a maximum retry count.
Dont: Do not retry immediately in a tight loop; it amplifies load on a struggling service.

Description: Propagate context deadlines across service boundaries so upstream timeouts cancel downstream work.
Do: Set a timeout on the context and pass it to every outbound call.
Dont: Do not use context.Background for outbound calls; it ignores upstream cancellation.

Description: Accept an idempotency key header and deduplicate requests so retries do not cause double processing.
Do: Store the idempotency key and result; return the cached result on duplicate requests.
Dont: Do not process the same request twice; network retries can cause duplicate side effects.

Description: Apply rate limiting to protect services from being overwhelmed by too many requests.
Do: Use golang.org/x/time/rate as middleware to limit requests per client or globally.
Dont: Do not allow unlimited request rates; one misbehaving client can degrade the entire service.

Description: Load secrets from HashiCorp Vault at runtime instead of baking them into config files or environment variables.
Do: Use the Vault Go client to read secrets at startup or rotate them periodically.
Dont: Do not store secrets in config files committed to version control.

Description: Listen for OS signals and gracefully shut down HTTP servers, message consumers, and database connections in order.
Do: Use signal.NotifyContext and shut down components in reverse initialization order.
Dont: Do not call os.Exit or log.Fatal during normal shutdown; it skips cleanup.
Code Good:
ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
defer stop()
srv := &http.Server{Addr: ":8080", Handler: mux}
go func() { srv.ListenAndServe() }()
<-ctx.Done()
slog.Info("shutting down")
shutdownCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 15*time.Second)
defer cancel()
srv.Shutdown(shutdownCtx)
consumer.Close()
db.Close()
slog.Info("shutdown complete")

Description: Use multi-stage Docker builds to compile Go binaries in a build stage and copy only the binary into a minimal runtime image.
Do: Use a Go builder stage and a distroless or scratch final stage.
Dont: Do not ship the Go toolchain or source code in production images.
Code Good:
# Dockerfile
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /app/server ./cmd/server

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
Code Bad:
FROM golang:1.23
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN go build -o server ./cmd/server
CMD ["./server"]
# 1GB+ image with Go toolchain and source code

Description: Define resource requests and limits plus liveness and readiness probes in Kubernetes deployment manifests.
Do: Set CPU and memory requests and limits and configure probe endpoints.
Dont: Do not deploy without resource limits; a single pod can consume all node resources.
Code Good:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-service
        image: order-service:v1.2.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 128Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 512Mi
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /readyz
            port: 8080
          periodSeconds: 5
Code Bad:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-service
        image: order-service:latest
        # No resource limits, no probes
        # Pod can consume entire node
        # K8s cannot detect unhealthy pods

Description: Authenticate service-to-service communication using mutual TLS or signed JWTs to prevent unauthorized access.
Do: Use mTLS certificates or verify JWTs on every inter-service call.
Dont: Do not allow unauthenticated internal traffic; a compromised service can access everything.

Description: Use contract tests to verify that service interfaces match expectations between producers and consumers.
Do: Write contract tests that verify request and response schemas between services.
Dont: Do not rely solely on end-to-end tests; they are slow and brittle in microservice architectures.
Code Good:
func TestOrderServiceContract(t *testing.T) {
    srv := httptest.NewServer(NewOrderHandler(mockOrderService{}))
    defer srv.Close()
    resp, err := http.Post(srv.URL+"/orders", "application/json",
        strings.NewReader(`{"item":"widget","qty":2}`))
    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Equal(t, http.StatusCreated, resp.StatusCode)
    var result map[string]interface{}
    json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&result)
    require.Contains(t, result, "id")
    require.Contains(t, result, "status")
    require.Equal(t, "placed", result["status"])
}
